content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 21, 1990


Pete R. Zaragosa
Summer Youth Jobs Coordinator
Contra Costa County
    Private Industry Council
2425 Bisso Lane, Suite 100
Concord, CA 94520

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-90-062

Dear Mr. Zaragosa:

    You are seeking advice on behalf of the Contra Costa County
Private Industry Council ("the industry council") regarding ap-
plication of the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act")¹ to the industry council's youth jobs program.
Because your request for advice does not refer to a specific
governmental decision, we are treating your request as a request
for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy en-
closed).²

    The following advice is based on the facts that you provided
in your letter of January 18, 1990 and in subsequent telephone
conversations with you and with Robert Whatford of your office.

QUESTION

    As part of its summer youth program, can the industry council
send out a mass mailing to private employers that contains the
names and signatures of a city mayor and member of the county
board of supervisors?


_______________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2, Di-
vision 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Govern-
ment Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-062
Page 2

CONCLUSION

The industry council may send the mailing containing the
names and signatures of a city mayor and member of the county
board of supervisors to private employers as part of its summer
youth program. The city or county may not pay the postage for the
mailing, nor incur expenses in excess of $50 with regard to the
costs of design, production, or printing of the letter.

FACTS

The federal Job Training Partnership Act provides funds to
the county for administration of an employment training program.
(The funds are allocated to the state, which distributes them to
the county.) The industry council functions as the governing body
for the employment training program for most of Contra Costa
County (the City of Richmond has its own program). Its governing
board is composed of representatives from private sector business,
the state office of education, the state rehabilitation depart-
ment, community based organizations, and local government. The
board does not include any elected officeholders.

The industry council receives funds from the private sector
to sponsor a "Summer Youth Jobs Program," a program designed to
find young people jobs in the community. The industry council
uses these funds to send a mailing to the business community which
encourages private employers to participate in this program by
making jobs available. The letter is targeted by area and
contains the names and signatures of the city's mayor, the
district's supervisor on the county board of supervisors, the lo-
cal chamber of commerce president, and the industry council chair.
The letter is copied onto respective city and/or chamber of com-
merce letterhead and mailed to the area's private employers.

ANALYSIS

Affiliation

Regulation 18901(a) (copy enclosed) provides that a mass
mailing may not be sent within the meaning of Section 89001 if,
among other things, the item sent either features an elected of-
ficer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mail-
ing (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(A)), or includes the name or other
reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, which is prepared in consultation
with the elected officer. (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B).)

In order for the mailing to fall within the mass mailing re-
strictions, the elected official must be affiliated with the
agency that sends the mailing. An elected officer is affiliated
with an agency when he or she is

... a member, officer, or employee of the agency or
a subunit such as a committee, or has supervisory

File No. I-90-062
Page 3

control over the agency, or appoints one or more
members of the agency.

Regulation 18901(c)(1)

No elected official sits on the industry council board, nor
exercises supervisory control over, nor appoints one or more
members to, the industry council. As such, the mayor and supervisor whose signatures appear on the mailing sent to local employers
are not "affiliated" with the industry council. If the industry
council is the agency which produces and sends the letters, then
the mayor and supervisor are not "affiliated" with the industry
council for purposes of Regulation 18901.³ (See Riddle Advice Letter, I-89-096, copy enclosed.)

Costs of design, production, and printing

Regulation 18901(a) also provides that a mass mailing may not
be sent within the meaning of Section 89001 if, among other
things, the costs of the mailing's distribution is paid for with
public funds (Regulation 18901(a)(3)(A)), or if the costs of the
mailing's design, production, and printing are paid for with public funds and exceed $50.00. (Regulation 18901(a)(3)(B).)

The industry council provides the body of its letter to cities
and chambers of commerce, who in turn copy the letter onto
their letterheads and mail them to the local private employers.

If the city pays to have the letters mailed, the city -- not
the industry council -- is the agency which "[produces or] sends
the letter onto the mailing," and the city's mayor is the featured elected official affiliated with the agency. (Regulation
18901(a)(3)(A).) Under these circumstances the cost of the mailing's distribution would be paid for with public funds. (Regulation 18901(a)(3)(A).) Because the mailings would be sent to the recipients at his or her business (Regulation 18901(a)(1)), the mailings would be
considered mass mailings under the Act and no more than 200 could
be sent in a single calendar month. (Regulation 18901(a)(4).)

If the industry council pays the postage for mailing the letters to the private employers, it -- and not the city -- is the
agency "which produces and sends" the mailing. The Act would not
consider the mailing to be "mass mailing" and subject the mailing to the various provisions of Section 89001 and Regulation
18901 provided the city incurred costs relating to the design,
production, and printing of the mailing, in an amount not in
excess of $50. (Regulation 18901(a)(3)(B)),

___________________________
3 This is so even if the mayor and supervisor, by having their
name and signature included in the letter, are "featured" (Regulation 18901(c)(2).)

File No. I-90-062
Page 4


I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this matter please contact me at (916) 322-5091.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Jonathan S. Rothman
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JSR:plh

Enclosures
